comments:
Summary:
The submission describes an industrial-scale fashion-product search system. Key components are: (1) a large internal fashion-attribute dataset (≈1M images, 19 categories, >90 attributes) with ROIs; (2) a custom vision encoder (ResCeption — inception-v3 modules augmented with residual/shortcut paths); (3) modeling multi-label attribute recognition as sequence generation with an LSTM to capture attribute dependencies; (4) a guided Faster R-CNN ROI detector that uses predicted (or user-provided) category guidance; (5) extraction of appearance (GoogleNet activations) and color features in ROIs, binarization for efficient hamming-distance retrieval, and an inverted-index over attributes to scale retrieval to millions of items. The paper reports some classification and captioning transfer results for the encoder, anecdotal retrieval examples, and claims of improved mAP for guided detection, but many referenced figures and tables are missing from the provided text and many experimental details are omitted.

Strengths:
- The paper tackles a real and practically important application (fashion product search) and describes an end-to-end system that integrates multiple components used in production.
- The idea of treating multi-label attribute prediction as sequence generation with an RNN/LSTM to capture label dependencies is sensible and aligns with recent work using sequence models for structured output.
- The guided-detection idea (using category guidance to reduce false positives) is practical and likely useful in e-commerce settings where meta-data or user intent is available.
- The engineering choices (inverted index on attributes, binary hashing for fast Hamming distance, combination of semantic attributes + visual features) are well motivated for scalability.

Weaknesses and concerns (substantial):
- Experimental evaluation is incomplete and under-specified. The manuscript repeatedly references tables/figures (Table 1/2/3, Fig. 2/3/5/6/8/9/10/11) that are not present in the provided text; quantitative claims (e.g., mAP improvements, attribute recognition precision/recall, retrieval accuracy on the 3M set) are not reported in a verifiable manner. This makes it impossible to judge the empirical contribution or to compare to reasonable baselines.
- Comparisons to prior work and baselines are very limited. While the system combines known modules (inception/residual, Faster R-CNN, LSTM, GoogleNet features), the paper does not present direct comparisons to strong baselines for attribute prediction (e.g., independent classifiers, classifier chains, structured prediction) or retrieval (visual-feature-only retrieval, existing hashing/indexing methods), nor ablations that quantify the contribution of each component (ResCeption vs. inception-v3, LSTM ordering importance, guided vs. unguided detection, binarization effect beyond a single mAP drop on Holidays). The only numbers given (e.g., ResCeption top-1/top-5 error, CIDEr for captioning) are either not contextualized or are tangential to the core retrieval problem.
- Many methodological details are missing: architecture/training hyperparameters for ResCeption and LSTM (depth, hidden size, learning rates, optimizers, batch size, data augmentation), how the attribute sequence ordering is determined for training/inference, whether beam search is used during generation, how multi-label outputs are converted to sets (stop criteria), exact implementation/training details of the guided Faster R-CNN, how the inverted index is formed and the retrieval pipeline implemented, hashing length and collision analysis, weighting between color and appearance distances, retrieval latency and memory usage in the reported 3M experiment.
- The dataset used for most evaluation is internal and not released; while industry datasets are common, that reduces reproducibility. No attempts to evaluate on common public benchmarks for product/image retrieval (or at least release an anonymized subset) were described.
- The writing is at times repetitive and contains typographic/formatting errors and duplicated paragraphs, which impede clarity. The submission appears to contain concatenated or repeated fragments, making it harder to follow.
- Claims of novelty are overstated. The components are largely known and the contribution appears to be an engineering integration and some practical innovations (guided detection, using an attribute-based inverted index). Without substantial experimental evidence, the originality and impact are limited.

Suggestions to improve the paper:
1) Provide complete experimental results: include Table 2/3 and all referred figures. Report quantitative metrics for (a) attribute prediction (precision/recall/F1 per attribute and overall), (b) ROI detection (mAP with numerical values, both guided and unguided), (c) retrieval performance on a held-out benchmark (e.g., mAP, precision@k) for the 3M reference set and ideally on public benchmarks or at least on a reproducible subset.
2) Add ablation studies: (i) ResCeption vs. inception-v3 (and possibly vs. ResNet), (ii) LSTM sequence modeling vs. binary-relevance or classifier-chains baselines for multi-label prediction to show gains from sequence modeling, (iii) guided vs. unguided detection, (iv) effect of binarization on retrieval performance as a function of hash length, (v) effect of the inverted index (speedups) and tradeoffs.
3) Clarify training and implementation details (architectures, hyperparameters, dataset splits, how sequences are formed, inference procedure, hashing dimensions), so the work can be reproduced or meaningfully compared.
4) Clean up the manuscript: remove duplicated paragraphs, ensure all references/tables/figures are present and correctly labeled, improve clarity and organization.
5) If possible, release a portion of the dataset or an evaluation protocol so others can benchmark against the system.
6) Provide a user or online-A/B evaluation to support claims that attribute-based retrieval better reflects user intent than visual-only approaches.

Recommendation rationale:
The paper describes a practical, end-to-end industrial system and presents several reasonable ideas (RNN-based multi-label modeling; guided detection; inverted-index over attributes). However, the lack of complete and rigorous experimental evaluation, missing baselines and ablations, and clarity issues prevent me from assessing scientific contribution and reproducibility. The work reads more like an engineering system report than a rigorously evaluated research contribution. With a more complete set of experiments, clearer writing, and stronger baselines, the work could be a useful contribution; in its current form I recommend a marginal score.
